summary: Joel Spolsky reflects on the universal struggle of programmer productivity, noting that even productive developers only get two to three hours of real coding done per day. He identifies that the hardest part isn't sustaining productivity but simply getting started — crossing the chasm between procrastination and flow. Drawing on his experience as an Israeli paratrooper, he introduces the military concept of 'Fire and Motion' as a metaphor for business strategy: you must keep moving forward every day while recognizing when competitors are firing at you just to pin you down. He argues that big companies like Microsoft use constant technology churn as cover fire to keep competitors busy reacting instead of innovating. For small companies, the winning strategy is simply to make progress every day and avoid getting distracted by others' volleys.
key_points: Productive developers typically get only two to three hours of real coding per day, yet still outperform peers who appear to work longer hours
The hardest part of productivity is getting started — once in flow, momentum sustains itself like a loaded bicycle at speed
The military principle of 'Fire and Motion' applies to business: move forward while forcing competitors to react to your volleys rather than advance
Big companies use constant technology churn (ODBC, DAO, ADO, ADO.NET, etc.) as cover fire to keep smaller competitors busy reimplementing instead of innovating
Small companies win by making incremental daily progress and ignoring checkbox features demanded by competitors' sales teams
money_quotes: What drives me crazy is that ever since my first job I've realized that as a developer, I usually average about two or three hours a day of productive coding.
For me, just getting started is the only hard thing. An object at rest tends to remain at rest.
It took me another fifteen years to realize that the principle of Fire and Motion is how you get things done in life.
You have to move forward a little bit, every day. It doesn't matter if your code is lame and buggy and nobody wants it.
The companies that do well are the ones who rely least on big companies and don't have to spend all their cycles catching up and reimplementing.
Microsoft is shooting at you, and it's just cover fire so that they can move forward and you can't, because this is how the game is played, Bubby.
Are you supporting it because your customers need it, or because someone is firing at you and you feel like you have to respond?
We just have to come in every morning and somehow, launch the damn editor.
key_insight: Productivity comes from making incremental forward progress every day and refusing to let competitors' technology churn distract you from building what matters.
